1:
"In the end of the sabbath, as it began to dawn toward the first day of the week, came Mary Magdalene and the other Mary to see the sepulchre." The witnesses give most of their labour to the chronology, then to the women and their errand, and to the mystery of the day.

The grammatical-historical and patristic voices reconcile the four accounts. Gill renders the phrase "the goings out of the sabbath," after sunset; Augustine explains that "evening" names "the whole night, in the end of which they come to the sepulchre," Scripture's "usual mode of speaking, to express the whole by a part." Bede orders it more plainly: they "prepared the spices... in the evening, but... took them to the sepulchre in the morning." Barnes and Ellicott set the visits in sequence — the women watched the burial, rested the Sabbath, bought spices when it was past, and "at earliest dawn... set out," reaching the tomb as the sun rose (Mark 16:2; Luke 24:1; John 20:1). Robertson alone reads epiphôskô ("dawn") of the Jewish day beginning at sunset, allowing an earlier visit "to see the sepulchre" distinct from the later spice-bearing — "why allow only one visit for the anxious women?"

The women are Mary Magdalene and "the other Mary," the mother of James and Joses, with Salome (Mark) and Joanna (Luke); the Virgin Mary, Henry notes, is not named, perhaps kept home by the beloved disciple. Matthew says they came "to see the sepulchre," the others "to anoint" — no contradiction, for "he who mentions a few does not deny that there are more" (Barnes).

The fathers find a mystery in the dawning. Bede observes that whereas day once went before night, "now most fitly night goes before day, when, through faith in the resurrection, we are brought back from the darkness of sin... to the light of life." Chrysologus reads the two Marys under one name as "the Church coming out of the two nations, the Gentiles and the Jews, and being yet one." Calvin, for the Reformed, calls this "the closing scene of our redemption," on which all faith hangs (1 Corinthians 15:14): Christ revealed it "gradually, according to their capacity," beginning with the women, whom he made "apostles of the apostles" — both to "chastise the indifference of the apostles" and to abase "the loftiness of the flesh," so that we "become fools" and "receive the testimony of the women."
2:
"And, behold, there was a great earthquake: for the angel of the Lord descended from heaven, and came and rolled back the stone from the door, and sat upon it." The witnesses treat the earthquake, the angel's descent, and the purpose of the rolled stone.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the verb as a pluperfect — "there had been a great earthquake" (Barnes, Gill) — before the women arrived; Ellicott notes "they inferred it from what they saw," and that this answered their question on the way, "Who shall roll away the stone for us?" (Mark 16:3), a task "beyond their strength." Robertson insists it is "clearly not the earthquake of 27:51," and that Matthew alone relates "the coming of the angel... who rolled away the stone and was sitting upon it." Gill hears in it "an emblem of the shaking of the earth by the preaching of the Gospel" and "a prelude of the general resurrection." Clarke stresses the angel "descended from God, out of heaven, for this very purpose" — no "ordinary messenger."

On the rolled stone the commentators agree it was not to free the Lord. "The stone was rolled away," says Robertson (with McNeile), "not to let the Lord out, but to let the women in to prove the fact of the empty tomb" — Bede's point that "He who... had power to enter the world through the closed womb of a Virgin... was able to depart out of the world by rising from a sealed sepulchre." The angel "sat upon it," a posture, the fathers say, of triumph over death.

Calvin, for the Reformed, gathers the theology. "By many signs the Lord showed the presence of his glory, that he might more fully prepare the hearts of the holy women to reverence the mystery"; since it was "no matter of little consequence to know that the Son of God had obtained a victory over death," "it was necessary to remove all doubts." The visible signs were given "that our minds may conceive of him as invisible," and "under bodily forms" we "obtain a taste of his spiritual essence." He adds the harmony: the three Evangelists, "from a desire of brevity," name one angel where Luke and John speak of two — "a part is taken for the whole." Robertson agrees the variations only "strengthen the independent witness to the essential fact that Jesus rose from the grave."
3:
"His countenance was like lightning, and his raiment white as snow." The witnesses dwell on the angel's terrifying brightness and the symbolism of his white robes.

The grammatical-historical commentators parse the words. "Countenance," Barnes notes, refers in the original "to his whole person... his general aspect," not the face only; Robertson observes the noun (eidea) occurs only here in the New Testament. The likeness to "lightning" marks him "peculiarly bright and shining" — the very flashing, Gill and Clarke suggest, that struck the keepers with terror. The "raiment white as snow" the commentators read as the dress of celestial beings (Barnes citing Acts 1:10; Daniel 7:9; Revelation 1:14), white being "among the Jews the symbol of purity, or innocence," and also "of joy and triumph."

Ellicott connects the description with two great visions — the Transfiguration, where Christ's raiment was white (Mark 9:3), and "the Ancient of Days" in Daniel 7:9 — so that the herald of the resurrection shares the glory of the risen Lord. Gregory's tradition (from the next verse) draws the moral force of the two images together: lightning "inspires terror," snow "is an emblem of equity," for the angel, like God himself, is "terrible to sinners and mild to the righteous."

Clarke turns the white robes to a pointed application. The angel "was clothed in garments emblematical of the glad tidings which he came to announce. It would have been inconsistent with the message he brought, had the angel appeared in black robes, such as those preposterously wear who call themselves his successors in the ministry of a once suffering, but now risen and highly exalted, Saviour." The brightness, in the witnesses' reading, is no mere ornament: it is the visible token of a heavenly nature and of the joy of the resurrection, fitted at once to terrify the guilty guard and to assure the seeking women that the messenger is from God. Henry's tradition adds that the angel's splendour represented "the glory of Christ, to which he was now risen" — the same description given at the Transfiguration — and bespoke too "the glory of the saints in their resurrection, when they shall be as the angels of God in heaven."
4:
"And for fear of him the keepers did shake, and became as dead men." The witnesses contrast the terror of the guard with the comfort soon given the women.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the irony that hardened soldiers should faint. They were "Roman soldiers and veterans, who had been used to terrible sights in the field of battle... and yet were seized with a panic" (Gill); "by terror they fainted, or were thrown into a swoon" (Barnes); Ellicott infers "the two Maries when they reached the sepulchre saw the soldiers prostrate in their panic terror," and Robertson adds, "no wonder that they became as dead men and fled before the women came." Clarke draws the double effect: "God can, by one and the same means, comfort his servants, and terrify his enemies"; the resurrection "is a subject of terror to the servants of sin, and a subject of consolation to the sons of God."

Calvin, for the Reformed, makes the verse a study in two kinds of fear. The Lord "struck the soldiers with terror, as if he had engraved their consciences with a hot iron, so as to constrain them reluctantly to feel his divine power" — a terror that "hindered them from treating with careless mockery the report of the resurrection," though they were soon "gained over by money" to deny it. He marks "the distinction between the two kinds of fear": the soldiers "were terrified, and... fell down like men who were almost dead; but no power was exerted to raise them"; "a similar terror seized the women, but their minds... were restored by the consolation which immediately followed."

"It is proper," Calvin concludes, "that the majesty of God should strike both terror and fear indiscriminately into the godly as well as the reprobate, that all flesh may be silent before his face. But when the Lord has humbled and subdued his elect, he immediately mitigates their dread... and by the sweetness of his grace heals the wound which he had inflicted." The reprobate, by contrast, "he either overwhelms by sudden dread, or suffers to languish" — for to the women alone did the angel say, "Fear not." So the same risen power, the witnesses agree, is the joy of his friends and the confusion of his foes (cf. Psalm 76:5).
5:
"And the angel answered and said unto the women, Fear not ye: for I know that ye seek Jesus, which was crucified." The witnesses dwell on the comfort given the seekers and the commendation in the angel's words.

The "Fear not ye" the commentators set against the trembling guard. Ellicott notes the women "had been asking... unuttered questionings and fears," and the angel "knew their distress and amazement at the sight of the emptied sepulchre, and told them that there was no cause for fear." Barnes reads "fear" widely — "be not agitated, or troubled, that you do not find the body of the Saviour" — and takes the scene inside the sepulchre, reconciling the accounts: Mark's "young man... on the right side," Luke's "two men," the angel called now "angel" and now "man" (for "angels... from appearing in the form of men are often called as they appear").

"I know that ye seek Jesus, which was crucified" the witnesses hear as both knowledge and commendation. Robertson notes the perfect participle (estaurômenon) — "state of completion. This he will always be," as Paul preached "Jesus Christ and him crucified" (1 Corinthians 2:2); and that, according to John, Mary Magdalene had already left to tell Peter and John (John 20:1), while the other women remained for this interview. Henry's tradition presses the assurance: "those that seek Jesus have no reason to be afraid... All our believing enquiries after the Lord Jesus are observed... in heaven."

Clarke turns the moment to the honour of the women: "speaking after the manner of men, these women deserved to be the first witnesses of the resurrection of Christ; during life they ministered to him, and in death they were not divided." Against the common notion "that women are more fickle and less courageous than men," he holds "the reverse... to be the truth, in those who are thoroughly converted to God." The crucified one whom they sought, the witnesses agree, they sought rightly — and were met not with reproof but with the tenderest reassurance. The angel mentions his being crucified, Henry's tradition notes, "the more to commend their love to him" — they sought him still, "though he was crucified," and indeed "because he was so"; true believers love and seek Christ not in spite of his cross but for it.
6:
"He is not here: for he is risen, as he said. Come, see the place where the Lord lay." The witnesses fasten on the resurrection announced, the appeal to Christ's own word, and the evidence of the empty tomb.

"He is risen, as he said" the commentators ground in Christ's own predictions, which the disciples had not grasped (Matthew 16:21; 27:63). Henry's tradition makes this the angel's proof "as the proper object of faith": "he said that he would rise... why should you be backward to believe that which he told you would be?" — so the angel "preaches no other gospel than what they had already received." Ellicott fixes the time "at or about sunrise," fitly, "the Resurrection of the Son of Righteousness coinciding with the natural day-spring" (cf. Luke 1:78).

"Come, see the place" the witnesses read as the appeal to the empty tomb. It was a new tomb, "in which no man had ever been laid" (John 19:41), so "there could be no deception" (Clarke); Barnes describes the niche cut in the sepulchre's rock where the body had lain; Ellicott adds the detail from John of "the linen clothes... and the napkin" (John 20:5–6).

Robertson makes the empty tomb the heart of the matter. "Jesus the Risen" is "the heart of the testimony of the angel to the women," what Paul bids Timothy never forget — "Jesus Christ risen from the dead" (2 Timothy 2:8). "It will not do to say that Jesus arose in spirit... though his body remained in the tomb. The empty tomb is the first great fact confronting the women and later the men. Various theories were offered then as now. But none of them satisfy the evidence." Henry's tradition draws the comfort: those who "seek Christ, and miss of finding him where they expected" may know "that he is risen," and must "seek him as one risen" — not with carnal thoughts, but spiritually, by faith in his word. They who "make pictures and images of Christ," Henry's tradition adds, "forget that he is not here, he is risen"; our communion with him must be of a heavenly mind, seeking "the things that are above," where the risen Lord now is.
7:
"And go quickly, and tell his disciples that he is risen from the dead; and, behold, he goeth before you into Galilee; there shall ye see him: lo, I have told you." The witnesses dwell on the commission, the message to Peter, and the appointed meeting in Galilee.

"Tell his disciples" — Mark adds "and Peter," which the commentators find tender. It was, says Barnes, "a kind message to Peter, who had so recently denied his Lord... it shows the tender love and remembrance of Jesus even for his unfaithful friends." Calvin agrees the singling out was "not because he was at that time higher in rank... but because his crime, which was so disgraceful, needed peculiar consolation to assure him that Christ had not cast him off." Clarke marvels that "these faithful women proclaim the Gospel to those who were afterwards to be the teachers of the whole human race."

"He goeth before you into Galilee" points to the appointed meeting (cf. Matthew 26:32). Ellicott notes "the words seem to point to a meeting in Galilee as the first appearance... and St. Matthew records no other," a fact he explains on the view that this is "a Gospel according to Matthew," representing his oral teaching. Robertson balances it: Jesus "did appear to the disciples in Galilee on two notable occasions" (John 21; Matthew 28:16–20), yet "appeared to various disciples in Jerusalem on this first great Sunday" too; "he merely made a definite appointment in Galilee which he kept."

"Lo, I have told you" the witnesses take as solemn confirmation. Calvin says the angel "gives his signature to the promise of Christ," in Mark merely recalling "the very words of Christ"; Wesley calls it "a solemn confirmation of what he had said." Henry's tradition draws the whole together: the women are "made, as it were, the apostles of the apostles," sent "quickly" because "public usefulness to others must be preferred before the pleasure of secret communion with God" — and bidden to carry, as the lepers of old (cf. Luke 24:34 for the disciples' later confession), tidings too good to keep.
8:
"And they departed quickly from the sepulchre with fear and great joy; and did run to bring his disciples word." The witnesses dwell on the mingled emotions, the haste, and the harmony of the accounts.

The "fear and great joy" the commentators read as a true touch of life. It was, says Robertson, "fear and ecstasy" (Mark's phrase); "these mingled emotions of ecstasy and dread need cause no surprise." Clarke quotes Terence on news "so unexpected" that "fear and joy are generally intermingled," and Barnes distinguishes the two — fear "at the wonderful scenes," joy because "the news was too good to be credited at once." Henry's tradition notes "it is said of their joy, it was great joy; it is not said so of their fear," for "holy fear has joy attending it." They "did run," Barnes adds, the city being "half a mile or more from the place."

On the order of events the commentators reconcile the Gospels. Calvin and Ellicott note that John relates an earlier return of Mary Magdalene, who ran to tell Peter and John of the supposed theft (John 20:2) before she saw the Lord (John 20:14); the others — the "other Mary" and Joanna (Luke 24:10) — remained, and to them, rejoined by Mary, Christ then appeared. The discrepancies, Ellicott observes, "show that the narratives were independent."

Calvin, for the Reformed, finds in the mingled feeling an imperfect faith. The women "were indeed gladdened by what the angel told them, but at the same time were struck so that they were held in suspense between joy and perplexity"; "if their faith had been strong, it would have given them entire composure." Christ therefore "exhibited a remarkable instance of compassion, in meeting them while they thus doubted and trembled, so as to remove all remaining doubt" — manifesting "his heavenly glory to a wretched woman," and so beginning the display of "a new and eternal life" where there was "nothing in the eyes of man but what was base and contemptible." The very haste of their running, Henry's tradition adds, was quickened by both feelings at once: "the fear and joy together added wings to their motion," for "where the heart is enlarged with the glad tidings of the gospel, the feet will run the way of God's commandments."
9:
"And as they went to tell his disciples, behold, Jesus met them, saying, All hail. And they came and held him by the feet, and worshipped him." The witnesses treat the greeting, the act of worship, and the reconciliation with John's "Touch me not."

"All hail" the commentators render literally "rejoice" (chairete) — "a term of salutation connected with the idea of joy" (Barnes), "probably our Lord's wonted greeting to the company of devout women" (Ellicott), by which "they at first recognised their Lord, as Mary Magdalene had done by His utterance of her own name." Robertson notes Jesus "came suddenly face to face with them" as they brooded on the empty tomb.

"Held him by the feet, and worshipped him" the witnesses read as prostrate homage. To "clasp His feet" (Ellicott) was "the usual posture of supplication" (Barnes, citing 2 Kings 4:37); Raban says Christ "offers His feet to be held by the women, to shew that He had real flesh, which can be touched." The worship, Barnes holds, "seems to denote the homage due to the Messiah risen from the dead... agreeably to John 5:23," though Ellicott cautions the prostration "does not necessarily imply a new form of homage" (cf. Matthew 8:2), the disciples being "perhaps not till later" led to feel it was "due to God and to the Man Christ Jesus, and to no other."

The apparent clash with "Touch me not" (John 20:17) the witnesses resolve. Calvin says the Lord "permitted her to touch... that nothing might be wanting to give her full conviction," but forbade the eager, "superstitious" clinging that "withheld her from perceiving" the design of his resurrection. Chrysologus reads the two scenes by Mary's two states — "that is of divine grace, this of human nature" — when "wise in human things, we are blinded." Cyril hears in the clasped feet the Bride's word, "I will hold him, and will not let him go" (Song of Solomon 3:4): "He was dead whom they sought, yet was not the hope of the Resurrection quenched."
10:
"Then said Jesus unto them, Be not afraid: go tell my brethren that they go into Galilee, and there shall they see me." The witnesses dwell on the calming of fear, the tender title "my brethren," and the renewed appointment.

"Be not afraid" the commentators read as the soothing of holy dread. The ancients in the presence of a divine being "were commonly struck with great fear" (Barnes, citing Luke 5:8); the women, "still afraid for joy and embarrassment" (Robertson), are calmed by Jesus, "the same Jesus with whom they had been before his death." Calvin calls their fear "improper," arising from amazement yet "opposed to the tranquillity of faith"; Christ bids them rejoice, for "we never know aright our Lord's resurrection, until... we venture to rejoice that we have been made partakers of the same life."

The title "my brethren" the witnesses find full of tenderness. It was, Clarke notes, "the first time our Lord called his disciples by this endearing name," and "in speaking thus, he gives them a full assurance... that all that was passed was as buried for ever." They are "brethren by spiritual relationship" (Ellicott, citing Matthew 12:49 and John 20:17, "my Father and your Father"; cf. Hebrews 2:11). Calvin marvels at "the astonishing kindness of Christ, in deigning to bestow the name of brethren on deserters who had basely forsaken him" — a name chosen "for the purpose of soothing the grief by which he knew that they were grievously tormented." Wesley puts it simply: "I still own them as such, though they so lately disowned and forsook me."

The renewed command to meet in Galilee, Calvin adds, served "again to collect and raise up the Church, which was scattered and fallen down"; and since the apostles were not the only "brethren," "this message was conveyed... in order that it might afterwards come to us," that we too "receive the fruit of it on the ground of our being related to him." Robertson notes that, though Matthew omits the special "and Peter" of Mark, "we may be sure that the special message to Peter was delivered."
11:
"Now when they were going, behold, some of the watch came into the city, and shewed unto the chief priests all the things that were done." The witnesses read the guards' report as the testimony of enemies to the resurrection.

The grammatical-historical and patristic voices alike seize the irony. Henry's tradition sets the two errands side by side: "when the women were going to bring that news to the disciples, which would fill their hearts with joy, the soldiers went to bring the same news to the chief priests, which would fill their faces with shame." Chrysostom presses that the very signs "were wrought for the soldiers' sake, that they might be stunned with amazement, and bear testimony to the truth": "when truth is proclaimed by its adversaries, it adds to its brightness." The soldiers, all agree, "told a truthful account as far as they understood it" (Robertson) — "all the things that were done," the earthquake, the opened tomb, the shining form (Ellicott, Barnes).

The fathers turn the report into a refutation of the theft that would soon be alleged. Chrysostom asks how "men poor, and of no station, and who scarcely dared to shew themselves" could steal the body past a sealed stone and a guard, and why, had they done so, they would have stripped off the myrrh-soaked grave-clothes; "their endeavours to conceal the Resurrection do but make it more manifest," for "when they say, His disciples stole the body, they confess that it is not in the sepulchre." Remigius drives it home: "if the guards slept, how saw they the theft? and if they saw it not, how could they witness thereto?"

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the soldiers' motive. Having found "their diligence was of no service to them," they "contrive a new method of cheating their employers out of their money," seizing "the cruel and implacable hatred which the priests bore towards Christ" and abusing "their evil conscience to extort money." So the report that should have led the priests "to penitence" (Jerome) became instead the occasion of a fresh and deeper crime. Raban marks the contrast of dispositions: "simple minds and unlearned country-folk often make manifest without guile the truth of a matter," while "a crafty wickedness studies how to recommend falsehood by glosing words" — and so the guilt of the blood they had imprecated upon themselves is now compounded by the purchase of a lie against the truth of the Resurrection.
12:
"And when they were assembled with the elders, and had taken counsel, they gave large money unto the soldiers." The witnesses describe the council's deliberation and its resort to bribery.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. The matter was grave enough to convene "the great council of the nation" (Barnes), "a formal, though probably... packed, meeting of the Sanhedrin" (Ellicott; cf. the like councils of Matthew 27:1), now possible since "the Sabbath was over" (Clarke). Ellicott adds a telling motive: "the chief priests were Sadducees, and therefore specially interested in guarding against what would appear as a contradiction of their main dogma" — the denial of the resurrection (cf. Acts 4:2).

Barnes lays out the dilemma that drove them. "Notwithstanding all their caution, it was plain that the body of Jesus was gone"; the disciples would affirm he was risen; Jesus himself might appear. Having taken such pains to prove him dead — convincing Pilate, setting a guard, sealing the stone — "they had shut themselves out from... the most plausible plea" that he had only swooned. "They concluded, after consultation, that but one way was left — to bribe the soldiers... and to attempt to convince the world that Jesus... had been really stolen."

"Large money" the witnesses note with irony. Robertson observes "these pious Sanhedrists knew full well the power of bribes," making "a contract with the Roman soldiers to tell a lie about the resurrection of Jesus as they paid Judas money to betray him." Henry's tradition marvels that men who "loved their money" were yet "very prodigal of it" to "carry on a malicious design against the gospel of Christ" — "they gave the soldiers, it is likely, as much as they asked." The same council that had bought the betrayal now buys the cover-up, showing, as the witnesses agree, "not the slightest tendency to be convinced by the facts though one had risen from the dead" (Robertson). Thus, Henry's tradition observes, "they who have sold themselves to work wickedness find that one sin draws on another," plunging them "into a wretched necessity of adding iniquity to iniquity" — for having put Christ to death, there was "no way of standing to what they had done, but by confronting the evidence of his resurrection."
13:
"Saying, Say ye, His disciples came by night, and stole him away while we slept." The witnesses fasten on the self-contradiction of the concocted story.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the absurdity at once. "If they were asleep," asks Wesley, "how do you know they did? And if ye were awake, why did you let the disciples steal him?" Robertson calls it "an Irish bull on the face of it. If they were asleep they would not know anything about it." Ellicott agrees the tale was "on the face of it self-contradictory": "all that they could know was that they had fallen asleep, and that when they awoke the sepulchre was open and empty."

Clarke marshals "a whole heap of absurdities." Is it likely "that so many men would all fall asleep, in the open air, at once"? Or "that a Roman guard should be found... asleep, when it was instant death... to be found in this state"? Could they "be so sound asleep as not to awake with all the noise... of removing the great stone, and taking away the body"? Was there time enough "to do all this... without being perceived"? And, decisively, "if they were asleep, how could they possibly know that it was the disciples that stole him... for, being asleep, they could see no person."

From this the witnesses draw a striking conclusion: the lie proves the very thing it denies. "From their own testimony," says Clarke, "the resurrection may be as fully proved as the theft." The disciples themselves, Clarke adds, were "few," "terrified," and "dispirited," with "the great danger of such a bold enterprise" against "armed men" — so improbable an account that, in the witnesses' reading, it could only have been believed by those who, for other reasons, were already resolved not to believe that Christ was risen. Nor, the witnesses note, were means of disproof ever attempted: the houses where the disciples lodged were never searched, no body was ever produced, and the apostles who proclaimed the resurrection in Jerusalem were never prosecuted for theft — silence which itself confesses that the priests did not credit their own report.
14:
"And if this come to the governor's ears, we will persuade him, and secure you." The witnesses note the soldiers' peril and the priests' promise to shield them, which itself betrays the lie.

The danger was real. To sleep on watch was, by Roman military law, punishable by death (cf. the fate threatened in Acts 12:19), so the soldiers needed assurance before they would confess such negligence. The priests undertake to "persuade him, and rid you of care" — the word, Robertson notes, meaning to make them "amerimnous" (free from anxiety): "they would try money also on Pilate and assume all responsibility." Barnes judges the promise easy to make, "for they knew... that Pilate could be easily bribed," and that, the feast over, he "would not be disposed himself to take any farther trouble about the matter."

Clarke reads the bargain as a mutual corruption: the priests "persuade him that it is for his own interest and honour to join in the deception," and "render you secure." The whole arrangement, Robertson adds, shows the soldiers "lived up to their bargain, and this lie lives on through the ages," Justin Martyr later accusing the Jews of spreading it.

Yet the very care taken for the soldiers' safety, Henry's tradition argues, "plainly gives the lie to the story." Had the guards truly slept and let the body be stolen, "the priests and elders would certainly have been the forwardest to solicit the governor to punish them for their treachery." That instead they labour to protect them proves the priests knew the soldiers had committed no real negligence — that there was no theft to punish. "They promise more than they can perform," Henry concludes, "who undertake to save a man harmless in the commission of a wilful sin"; they could secure the soldiers "from the sword of Pilate's justice," but not "from the sword of God's justice, which hangs over the head of those that love and make a lie." Those who "undertake to save a man harmless in the commission of a wilful sin," Henry concludes, "promise more than they can perform"; and the soldiers, content to barter the truth for a coin and a covering, became the instruments by which the rulers sought to bury a fact that no bribe could finally suppress.
15:
"So they took the money, and did as they were taught: and this saying is commonly reported among the Jews until this day." The witnesses note the lie's spread and turn to the cumulative evidence for the resurrection.

The soldiers, "being men of no religion or conscience, were tempted with the money, and took it" (Gill), and "reported every where what had been put into their mouths." The "until this day," the commentators note, marks an interval — "about thirty years" (Barnes), or "at least fifteen or twenty" (Ellicott) — between the event and the writing; Ellicott and Calvin add that Justin Martyr found the report still current, "the Jews having sent chosen men into all parts of the world to propagate it."

Barnes uses the close of the chapter to marshal "the strongest evidence" for the resurrection. Christ had foretold it (Matthew 12:40); his death was beyond doubt; the tomb was guarded and sealed; yet the body was gone, which "the high priest did not dare to call in question." The theft-story is "intrinsically absurd" — sixty guards under penalty of death, a few timid disciples, the orderly grave-clothes (John 20:6), the impossibility of witnessing a theft while asleep. Against it stands the disciples' testimony, given in Jerusalem before the very council, sealed by lives of suffering and martyrdom for "no motive... but the love of truth"; and the world believed — three thousand Jews at Pentecost (Acts 2:41), and in three hundred years the whole Roman empire.

Calvin, for the Reformed, reads the lie's currency as judicial blinding: "it was the finishing stroke of the vengeance of God to blind the Jews, that the resurrection of Christ was buried by the perjury of the soldiers, and that so gross a falsehood was believed." Those who would not believe "were deceived by a voluntary error." Yet, he ends in triumph, "though this falsehood obtained currency among the Jews, this did not prevent the truth of the Gospel from flying at liberty to the very ends of the earth, as it always rises victorious over all the obstacles in the world."
16:
"Then the eleven disciples went away into Galilee, into a mountain where Jesus had appointed them." The witnesses dwell on the obedient eleven, the mountain, the meaning of Galilee, and the place of this scene among the appearances.

The "eleven" are noted, "for one had already perished" (Bede). They obey "the command of their Master" (Bede), keeping the appointment Christ had made before his death (Matthew 26:32). Henry's tradition presses the obedience: though it "seemed a needless thing to go into Galilee, to see him whom they might see at Jerusalem... they had learned to obey Christ's commands," and "our communion with God in secret must not supersede our attendance on public worship," this being "a public and general meeting" (the five hundred of 1 Corinthians 15:6).

The mountain the commentators take to be "probably the same... on which he was transfigured" (Henry; Wesley names Tabor). Bede reads it mystically: Christ "appeared to them in the mountain to signify that His Body... He had by His Resurrection exalted above all earthly things," and to teach the faithful to "pass from low pleasures to high desires"; for they were now to see him, Ellicott adds, not "in the body of His humiliation" but "in the body of His glory" (cf. Philippians 3:21). Galilee, "interpreted revelation" (Bede), fits the risen Lord and the turn to the nations: he "who before His Passion had said, Go not into the way of the Gentiles (Matthew 10:5), now... says, Go and teach all nations," silencing the Jews "who say that Christ's coming is to be for their salvation only."

Augustine, harmonising, shows "this manifestation to the eleven disciples on the mountain in Galilee took place last of all" — not on the day of the resurrection, nor within the eight days, since the appearance by the Sea of Tiberias was "the third"; he counts "in all ten distinct appearances," noting that "all is not written," for Christ had "much conversation with them during forty days" (Acts 1:3). Calvin observes that Matthew, "passing by those occurrences" the others record, "has selected what was of the greatest importance to us, namely, that when Christ appeared to the disciples, he likewise commissioned them to be apostles, to convey into every part of the world the message of eternal life."
17:
"And when they saw him, they worshipped him: but some doubted." The witnesses dwell on the act of worship, the identity of the doubters, and the value of their doubt.

"They worshipped him" the commentators take as "divine adoration, as the eternal Son of God... declared to be by his resurrection from the dead" (Gill); they "fell prostrate at His feet" (Ellicott), as the women had done (v.9). The act, Ellicott notes, "was not new in itself," but Christ's risen manifestations "had made the faith of the disciples stronger and clearer" (cf. John 20:28), "and so the act acquired a new significance."

"But some doubted" — the witnesses divide over who. Clarke takes it of "Thomas only at first" (cf. John 20:25), or simply that "they did not all believe at that time." Robertson and Henry's tradition refer it rather to others present beyond the eleven — for Paul says "over five hundred were present" (1 Corinthians 15:6) — "it is natural that some should hesitate... at the first appearance." Calvin allows either, suggesting "the remains of their former terror led them again into hesitation... until Christ made a nearer and more familiar approach."

The doubt itself the commentators turn into evidence. Barnes finds it proof "that they were honest men — that they were not easily imposed on — that they had not previously agreed to affirm that he had risen... that they were convinced only by the strength of the evidence." "Their very doubt," says Robertson, "makes it easier for us to believe"; and Henry's tradition adds that, the doubts being afterward removed and their faith grown "to a full assurance," "it tended much to the honour of Christ that the disciples doubted before they believed; so that they cannot be said to be credulous." Ellicott explains the hesitation by "something mysterious and supernatural in the manifestation of the glorified body — outlines at first indistinct and scarcely recognised" (cf. John 21:4; Matthew 14:26), "the more devoted and loving disciples" being "the first to recognise their Lord." Calvin adds that the same cause which "suddenly caused them to doubt" then "led them to worship," namely that Christ "had laid aside the form of a servant, and had nothing in his appearance but what was heavenly"; and Henry's tradition observes that the doubts, "afterward removed," grew "to a full assurance," so that the disciples "first questioned, and proved all things, and then held fast that which was true."
18:
"And Jesus came and spake unto them, saying, All power is given unto me in heaven and in earth." The witnesses dwell on the drawing near, the claim of universal authority, and the sense in which it was "given."

That Christ "came and spake" the commentators read as the removal of doubt. He "did not stand at a distance, but came near" (Henry), "drew near to them" so that "they were fully persuaded" (Clarke); Robertson pictures him speaking "as one already in heaven with a world-wide outlook and with the resources of heaven at his command."

"All power" — better "all authority" (pasa exousia, Robertson) — the witnesses take as the Mediator's universal dominion. Barnes explains the universe is put under him "more particularly as Mediator, that he might redeem his people... and bring them off conquerors" (cf. Ephesians 1:20–23; 1 Corinthians 15:25); Remigius hears the Psalmist's "Thou madest him to have dominion over the works of thy hands" (Psalm 8:6). Henry calls it "the great foundation of the Christian religion," asking "whence" he has it (given by the Father, Daniel 7:14) and "where" (heaven and earth, the whole universe).

The "given" raised the old question of Christ's equality with the Father. Wesley answers crisply: "All power is given to me, even as man. As God, he had all power from eternity." Calvin agrees the claim is not "the eternal power with which he was endued before the creation of the world, but to that which he has now received, by being appointed to be Judge of the world" — "in our flesh... in the person of the Mediator." Ellicott notes the aorist "was given," marking the exaltation "as at the moment of the Resurrection, and as the crown of His obedience unto death" (cf. Philippians 2:9); Robertson calls it "a timeless aorist." Clarke, on the other side, presses that to claim "all the authority in heaven and earth" without "impiety and blasphemy" is to "in the fullest manner assert his Godhead, and his equality with the Father." The witnesses thus agree the boundless authority, exercised through the Mediator, is the warrant on which the whole commission rests — for, as Calvin says, the apostles could never have undertaken "so arduous an office" but for knowing "that their Protector sitteth in heaven."
19:
"Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost." The witnesses expound the universal commission, the making of disciples, and the Triune name of baptism.

"Go ye therefore" rests on the authority just claimed — "because all power is mine, go" (Barnes); the apostleship, Calvin insists, "is not an empty title, but a laborious office," so that "no man can be a successor of the apostles who does not devote his services to Christ in the preaching of the gospel." The reach is now universal: "all nations" means "the Gentile nations of the world, as distinguished from the people of Israel" (Ellicott, Robertson), so the word "broke down the middle wall of partition" (Henry; cf. Ephesians 2:14) and reversed the earlier "Go not into the way of the Gentiles" (Matthew 10:5) — a universality, the commentators note, recorded fittingly in the Gospel written for Jews.

"Teach all nations" is, more exactly, "make disciples of" them (matheteusate, so Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson, Wesley) — "evangelism in the fullest sense" (Robertson), the disciples now becoming "in their turn... the teachers of others" (Ellicott). To "baptize... in the name" means, says Barnes, to be "devoted to" the Triune God, as Israel was "baptized unto Moses" (1 Corinthians 10:2); the "name," Ellicott adds, is "a revelation of what He is" (cf. Exodus 3:14).

The Triune form the witnesses make a proof of the Godhead of the Son and Spirit. "The union of these three names," says Barnes, "proves that the Son and Holy Ghost are equal with the Father," for "nothing would be more... blasphemous than to unite the name of a creature... with the name of the ever-living God in this solemn rite" (so Ellicott of the single "name," not "names"; cf. 2 Corinthians 13:14). The baptisms "in the name of the Lord Jesus" in Acts (Acts 2:38; Acts 19:5) they reconcile: it sufficed for converts "of the house of Israel" to confess Jesus as Messiah, while the heathen, who "had not known the Father," needed the full formula (Ellicott; Lightfoot to like effect). Calvin draws the Reformed inference for infants: since the covenant runs, "I will be thy God, and the God of thy seed after thee" (Genesis 17:7), "they who entered by faith into the Church of God are reckoned, along with their posterity, among the members of Christ," so "it is not rash to administer baptism to infants" — Clarke adding that the apostles, knowing the Jews baptized proselytes' children, "could not... have understood our Lord differently."
20:
"Teaching them to observe all things whatsoever I have commanded you: and, lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world. Amen." The witnesses dwell on the matter of the teaching, the great promise of presence, and the close of the Gospel.

The teaching is to comprehend "all things... not only baptism, but the Lord's supper; all positive institutions, and moral duties," and that "they may put them into practice" (Gill). The commentators stress that it must be Christ's commands only: ministers "should take care that they teach not human creeds and confessions of faith, in place of the Sacred Writings" (Clarke). Calvin makes this the verse's guard against the Pope — Christ "does not entirely resign his office," but sends the apostles "that they shall not bring forward their own inventions, but shall purely and faithfully deliver... what he has entrusted to them"; teachers must "depend on the mouth of the Master alone, so as to gain disciples for him, and not for themselves." Ellicott notes the words cover even "much unrecorded teaching" (cf. Matthew 10:27; John 13:34), and Robertson presses "the full value of what we now call religious education."

"Lo, I am with you alway" the witnesses make at once a proof of divinity and a promise of grace. Barnes argues "this also proves that Christ is Divine," for "no finite creature could thus be present with many men scattered in different parts of the world." Henry's tradition notes the "I am" (ego eimi), the prophetic present by which Christ, like God to Moses (cf. Exodus 3:14), speaks as "God, to whom past, present, and to come, are the same." Calvin explains the presence "spiritually... by the grace of his Spirit, as if he stretched out his hand from heaven"; and it is constant — "all days, every day," in "weakness, sorrows, joy, power" (Robertson), there being "no day, no hour... in which our Lord Jesus is not present with his churches and with his ministers."

"Unto the end of the world" — better "of the age" (Ellicott, Clarke) — the commentators extend beyond "the apostolic age" to "the end of time," the promise reaching "all the ministers of the gospel in the several ages of the church" (Henry). The "Amen" Clarke judges a copyist's addition, while Henry's tradition hears in it Christ's confirmation and "the church's concurrence... So be it, blessed Lord." Ellicott reflects on the abrupt close — no Ascension recorded — concluding the promise of "perpetual presence" was felt as "the fitting close of the record." So, as Robertson says, "Matthew's Gospel closes in a blaze of glory": the Risen Christ, "all powerful," sends his people to the world's farthest limits with the assurance that he is "with us all the time."
